speaker: interviewer
text: Miss Khane Baltser-- correction in the spelling-- the spelling of Baltser is B-A-L-T-S-E-R. Today is November 20, 1995-- Davie, Florida, USA. Let's get back and talk about the conditions in the ghetto. You were-- how did you take care of the health problems?

speaker: interviewee
text: At the health problems? I can't take care, because my sister, she helped me a lot. My father's youngest sister, she helped me a lot. And my father's youngest sister, she was-- I-- I talked to you before about that. She was in the partisan company what's to help kids to go out from ghetto. They open a special school for kids. And we was learning Lithuanian to talk correct in Lithuanian. Because before-- this was in 1942 and 1943-- kids, they was in Lithuanian families. And the Lithuanian polizei, they find out the kids, they can't talk in-- in Lithuanian, and they kill the kids. And they kill the Lithuanian families.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then they start to teach us to talk correct and be very careful. I was learning about one year Lithuanian. I was reading a lot in Lithuanian. And in 1944, in the beginning, my aunt, she took me to a Lithuanian family. And this family, they help me to survive. And I stayed there in this family to 1994, in the end, when the Russia army, they-- [NON-ENGLISH]? They liberated.

speaker: interviewee
text: Liberated this small town where I was, in Lithuania.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, how long did you stay with that Lithuanian family?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was stayed there about six months. But this was very difficult when I was there, because a lot of people, they notice I am a kid from another-- from another family. And a lot of Lithuanians, they will find out from what family, from what town I came, and-- but I was spoking very nicely in Lithuanian. And my Lithuanian family, they did not have problem about me. But they survive four kids. The partisan-- the-- the company-- the partisan-- what they help 32 kids-- girls, only girls. Because boys, they can't help them. This was difficult for boys, to help them.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the family that you-- the Lithuanian family that you stayed with?

speaker: interviewee
text: This family-- this name from the family was Juza Federavicius and Jonas Federavicius. This was a husband and a wife. They was-- was very nice to us, to the kids. They was like a father and a mother. And in this meantime, I called them mother and father. And after, when we was [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Liberated.

speaker: interviewee
text: Liberated, we was like a family. They did not never have kids. And the four kids, was they liberated, they was very warm to them. And we was warm to-- to this family, to the Lithuanian.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now let's get back to the ghetto. Did the Jewish community organize itself in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, they do. They help with us with food, with clothes, with-- to survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Now, you said that there was an underground in the-- in the ghetto. Was there any uprising or any resistance from the Jewish people or from the underground in the-- in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't understand this-- this--

speaker: interviewer
text: Or did any people, any Jews in the ghetto, that revolted against the Germans?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Not in Lithuania. They was working. People thinking it was a help-- the-- the Germans. But it's not true. Because I know-- I know exactly. Because my aunt, she was in this partisan company. And always, the Jewish polizei, they help the-- the-- the partisan to-- they tell them before what to do, because they-- to not to be like in-- how can I-- in [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: In a hole.

speaker: interviewee
text: In a hole. In a hole.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now inside the ghetto itself, were there any Jews that revolted, that-- what-- when they weren't-- were they unhappy? Did they do anything against the Germans in the ghetto itself?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. We have a couple people, were they-- fighting with the Germans. They shooting in the Germans. One man was named [NON-ENGLISH]. I remember only the name. And he was killed a couple Germans soldiers. And I-- I remember one other two Lithuanian polizei, and they--

speaker: interviewer
text: Hanged him.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hang him. And he would stay six days. And the Germans and the Lithuanian polizei, they-- everybody they talked to-- to the Jewish people-- go and see what we do when you will shoot in Germans other in Lithuanian polizei.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know how he got the gun to shoot the Germans?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I don't know. Nobody knows.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, you said the Jewish police helped the partisan. Did they also help the people in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, they helped the people in the ghetto. When the Germans came in-- in ghetto with buses to take people to killed, they was talking to everybody. Go, go and [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Hide. Hide.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hide, because maybe you can survived. This-- this is expect to you what you can do for yourself.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about the schools. Were there any schools for children in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Any schools, but we have a school the same like in Hebrew, for-- for boys. And the-- the kids, they are the same. They have the same problem to look out. It's not the Germans. It's not the soldiers. It's not the Lithuanian polizei. And the kids, they like to-- to learning and to-- to remember. And every Friday, they have in the synagogue, the same-- the same serve the-- the Jewish-- the Jewish religion about Friday, about Saturday. And everybody do what he can do.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you have the schools? Where-- where did the-- was the schools held?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was special apartment. Somebody tell them, OK, today we-- you came to my apartment. Tomorrow we came to yours apartment. And every day, somebody-- they-- they change the places. We did not have one place, special for the boys. But the Lithuanian, for the kids, we have a special place, one place only.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, how many children, more or less, were there in-- in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: They have a lot of children, but the mothers, they never-- like when the kids get out of sight. The kids was always in sight.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it possible to go out of the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because kids-- the soldiers-- what they was watching the ghetto around. They-- sometimes there was shoot the kids. Others, they grabbed the kids. And they killed them.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Now, was it the-- were you able, or the older people-- were they able to get out of the ghetto-- come back into the ghetto? Was-- was the ghetto with a fence closed in?

speaker: interviewee
text: The ghetto was closed. They have a special-- like a-- a big door. And when you go out from one side, it's the Jewish polizei. From the other side, it's the Lithuanian polizei. One are the two German soldiers. And when the-- somebody can text-- understand Lithuanian, the Jewish polizei, they explain to them what to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were they dealing with the-- with the rabbis-- the Germans or the Lithuanian police?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was, in the beginning, when the Germans started the war. This was in 1941. A lot of Lithuanian people-- I don't-- I can't say-- they was like bandits. They killed a lot of rabbis. But they-- they--

speaker: interviewer
text: Cut.

speaker: interviewee
text: --cut them-- the heads. And the head, they took on the garbage bags. And they was laughing. And they talked to-- to people, look what he can now teach you.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any people that escaped from the ghetto-- that run away from the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, a lot of people-- a lot of organization from the partisan, and families, Lithuanian families, where they carried off. They know this was like friend before the war. They help them. And from the organization, kids-- we was 32 kids. We was survived. This is thanks to the organization, to the partisan organization.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now this-- all this was in the Kovno ghetto, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is all in the Kovno ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any other people? Did the Germans bring any other Jews from other places?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. They bring people from French. I have pictures of them. From other countries, I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: But I remember one friends. I was walking with my girlfriend in-- in-- in the street. And we saw outside in ghetto, with the-- from the [NON-ENGLISH]. How you say in English, [NON-ENGLISH]? What is-- what is around the ghetto? Oh the-- from the fence.

speaker: interviewee
text: From the fence. And we saw people the same in-- in the yellow--

speaker: interviewer
text: Star. Star.

speaker: interviewee
text: Stars. And they ask us, where are you? We tell them, this is a ghetto. And they was thinking they came in the ghetto. But they go-- they didn't took them in the ghetto. They took them to the nine-- the Fort Nine, where they killed a lot of Jews-- people from Kovno.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the Fort Nine? Can you-- can you describe?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a special-- a big-- a field.

speaker: interviewer
text: Field.

speaker: interviewee
text: A-- a field. And they do-- did the-- dig-- dig--

speaker: interviewer
text: Digging. Digging?

speaker: interviewee
text: Digging? And they-- they-- they put in the-- the people when they kill them, when they shoot them, and where they are in life. They don't care. They took the people. And later on, they put it on the ground of the people. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned before about the German action-- actions. Can you describe what they were? How did they do it?

speaker: interviewee
text: The ghetto was a big place. We have different streets. When the Germans and the Lithuanian polizei, they came in, they cut up a couple streets from right side. From left side, they do anything to somebody else. But the people was-- they was in the right side. They took them in the-- in the buses. And later, they took them to the fort number nine to kill them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know? Did the people in the ghetto know about Fort Nine?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody knows.

speaker: interviewer
text: Nobody knew.

speaker: interviewee
text: Later, in 1944, a lot of people knows about this place.

speaker: interviewer
text: How often did they have those actions?

speaker: interviewee
text: The actions, they do this every two months, sometimes two times in the month, when it's came in a Jewish holiday. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Do any people that you know died from the actions?

speaker: interviewee
text: From my family died about 50 people in the action, a lot of friends, a lot of my friends' friends. It's difficult to tell how many people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know what was going on outside the ghetto-- what was going on with the war? Did you have any news?

speaker: interviewee
text: We know the-- first, in the first time, we did not know nothing. We know in 1943-- this was in-- in-- in July, in August-- we know was the Russian army, they pushed the Germany's army--

speaker: interviewer
text: Back.

speaker: interviewee
text: --back. And then the Lithuanians start to help Jewish families.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know about this? How did you get the news?

speaker: interviewee
text: We hearing about-- the teacher told us in school what is coming the time to be-- fry?

speaker: interviewer
text: Free.

speaker: interviewee
text: Free.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Now, how did the Germans react to the Russians pushing the army back?

speaker: interviewee
text: We don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: You don't know that.

speaker: interviewee
text: We don't know that.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you know that the-- the war was almost over?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in the Lithuanian family, and in the evening time, we-- we know. We was hearing it shooting, and the-- and the-- and the noises from the-- from the-- from the bombs, from everything. And in the-- in the-- maybe about 6, 7 o'clock, it was a little dark. And we saw like soldiers, but we don't know what kind of soldiers-- German soldiers, the Russian soldiers. And this was Russian soldiers came in in this small town where I was.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, when you were in the ghetto, you were the whole time with your sister? Your brother-in-law?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was all with this-- my sister, my-- my youngest brother. And when I go out from ghetto in 1944, I left them in ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, what happened to them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Later, in 1944, this was in March 26, I was not in ghetto. But I know from my Lithuanian family, they tell me about this terrible things, what the Germans to-- do to the kids in ghetto. Three days they grab kids from every single place, from every-- from any apartment. And they killed the kids and the oldest people. In this meantime, they killed my brother. He was 13. And my sister's baby, she was 2 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Please what you--

speaker: interviewer
text: What year? When did this happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in 1944 in March 28-- 26 to 28-- two days.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was almost the end of the-- of the war in-- in Lithuania.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was a special-- they do this special to kill kids. And the-- the rest for the people, they took them in Germany. My sister, she was-- they-- they took her to the-- to Germany. She was-- and stood-- and stood over them-- and stood guard. And I don't know exactly in what place she was.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was the beginning of the liquidation of the--

speaker: interviewee
text: This--

speaker: interviewer
text: --of the Kovno ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, this was the-- the beginning when they close the ghetto. But a lot of people stay in ghetto. They have bunkers, and they was there. And the Germans, they know what people, they was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Hiding. Hiding.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hided. And there was-- fired the ghetto. And it was a big fire in this mountain. The ghetto was two weeks--

speaker: interviewer
text: Burning.

speaker: interviewee
text: Burning. And everybody was there--

speaker: interviewer
text: Died.

speaker: interviewee
text: Died.

speaker: interviewer
text: The people that died are-- were the one that were hiding in the bunkers.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the-- the other people?

speaker: interviewee
text: The other people was in Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Germany.

speaker: interviewee
text: They took to Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who made this-- the decision for you to go and live with the Lithuanian family?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was my-- the partisan, the-- the company what-- what they have the special-- a big-- a lot of people were to help kids to survive them.

speaker: interviewer
text: To survive. When were you liberated?

speaker: interviewer
text: By whom?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Lithuanian family.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, who liberated you?

speaker: interviewee
text: After-- after the-- the--

speaker: interviewer
text: End of war.

speaker: interviewee
text: --oh. After, when I came back to Kovno. I was from Kovno about six, seven hours to drive with the car. This was a small town. This was not like in the city. This is a small town. And my Lithuanian family, she bring me to Kovno. And she find out what-- in the big synagogue-- we have a big synagogue in-- in Kovno-- what people, where they survived, they need to have the names, the-- the people. And was came back a lot of kids. And then the people at the organization, the same partisan organization-- they was-- I will tell you about the partisan. They was go out from ghetto in 1944 in-- before the-- the action of the kids. And-- and they was fighting with the Germans and the Lithuanian polizei in the [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: In the woods.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the?

speaker: interviewer
text: Forest.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the forest. And when it's finished, the war, they came back-- not everybody, but a lot of people came back. About 200 people was back. And the organization do what kind peoples came-- survived, except the kids. And when they have a lot of kids-- about 10 of the 12 kids-- we was thinking that it was a lot of kids. They open a special kinderheim.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Orphanage.

speaker: interviewee
text: The orphanage. And little for little, it came back about 30, 40, 50 kids. Kids was-- was in Russia. The father and the mother, they was in Kovno. And-- because this was a big panic. Nobody knows what to do. Somebody go on outside, they grab kids, and they took them to different places. They don't know who kids is that, but they like to help them. And the kids, when they came back from-- to Kovno, the fathers and the mothers, they was killed. And the old kids, they hold it in this place to help them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now you were in that orphanage?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in the-- was there. And later on, they open a Yiddish school for us. They're learning us only Yiddish. And they try to-- to be happy to us. And they try to tell us, you must be happy. You survived. And later we do a lot of things. We was open songs, clubs, and to play on the piano-- to be happy.

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] How long did you stay in the orphanage?

speaker: interviewee
text: Two years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, do you have any hobby-- any hobbies that you did while you were in ghetto, or in the orphanage?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I was in ghetto, when we have the school to learning Lithuanian, we have a couple songs in ghetto too. In ghetto, the people, they like to remind the people about happiness. And they do the words what happening in ghetto. And we was kids. We grew up like that. And we would sing songs in the school, in ghetto--

speaker: interviewer
text: In ghetto

speaker: interviewee
text: --a lot of songs. And after the war, we was-- can-- we was-- have a big club. And we talked to people. And we explained to people. And we sung to them a lot of Yiddish songs from ghetto.
